speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: January 27, 1995. The survivor is Hellmuth Szprycer. The interviewer is Hershey Felder. The city is Krakow in Poland. And the language of the interview is English. Please tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hellmuth Szprycer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Hamburg, on the 8-8-29. Can you tell me a little bit about where you were born and what it was like at the time? Yes, I was born in Hamburg, I lived there only until the age of three. I had two sisters, one, Lotti, two years older than me, and one Gisella, four years older than me. And we moved later on, when I was three years, old to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about life living in Germany and in Berlin for the time that you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Berlin from the time-- from probably six, seven years of age. We had a beautiful family, a kosher family, Yiddish family, from-- my grandfather was very firm. Actually, I was brought up, we brought up by our grandparents. My mother was in Berlin, but she-- we-- my father didn't know. That happens every day in life. And my grandparents lived in [NON-ENGLISH], by [NON-ENGLISH]. Very good district-- and my grandfather was well-known from Yid Synagogue. Everyday, it was a kosher household. My grandmother cooked beautiful big-- big cakes, You know, like cheese cakes and all that-- she had to do all herself. That was until my young age, six, seven years of age. I had a beautiful life. I went to school, Jewish school in the beginning. Eventually, it was stopped, but until eight, nine years of age, 10 years, I went to Jewish school. My sisters, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gisella, also, and Lotti, as well. But the tragedy started because, you've got to remember, I was born under the nation-- under Hitler's what's it called, and so this way, Hitler came up. So it was already nationalism in Germany, anti-Semitism. As a child, you don't feel it so hard, because you go down. When you come back from school, you play in the streets with your neighbors, which are not Jewish. And life went on. Child only sees the happiness. But they-- so, you know, I came to about 10 years of age. One of my sisters, Lotti, she got tuberculosis. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very ill, she became very ill. My mother came, because she lived somewhere else, but she came and wanted to take her to a sanatorium. And the Germans wouldn't allow it at moment, already. Being a Jewish girl, there was no saving to be done already at that time, and they couldn't cure her. So that-- Lotti died at the age of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, yes. That was already the biggest loss for us. I can remember my grandmother. She had-- the week before she died, she was lying in her bed. She probably weighed 30 kilos. And she knew. My grandmother slept with her the last week of her life to be near her. When the day happened that she died, what happened on at our house is unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they put her into the-- they cleaned her up, how it's done properly. And she was put into the little box there for a day. And she looked like a beautiful girl, a young Yiddish girl, I saw so beautiful, being that already. I remember, when that happened, I was in the Jewish school, in Munchener Strasse, where the synagogue was. And there was also where we could play after the school. And they called me, Lotti is dying. And must have been 15 minutes away or something-- I was running like mad. And I was standing next to the bed-- my grandfather, my grandmother, and Gisella. And she stood up in the bed, and she looked at all of us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then she fall down, and she was gone. When they took out of the house, my grandmother wanted throw herself out of the window, when they took the-- the-- how do you call it, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Coffin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the coffin, out of-- she looked through the windowm and she went my Lotti, my Lotti. That was already the first, what's it called, hit, which hit us strong in our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think this is-- is this something you can relate to be specifically because Germans forbade cure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. They also stopped schools then-- soon after that, Jewish schools. Yes, because they hated the Jews. I mean, under Hitler's-- beginning, '33, later, when he became in power officially, then he was already strongly in. But, before that, the voting and the SR and the SS went with him, it was going on all the time when I was the child. And a matter of fact, where we were living, our neighbors, some of them were very anti-Semitically. And they didn't like us, but they didn't show it before Hitler was voted in '39, before the war started, '38. Then they became more, what's it called, more obvious. But, anyhow, she was buried, in Weissensee. She got a grave there. We went together there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after-- soon after that, in '36, I remember that a lot of people immigrated because they felt that Hitler is coming, and there's nothing to stop that. So my mother, being stateless, she married my father who was Polish, she had to leave Germany in '36, and leave us children behind-- the two of us now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she? Where was she sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to England, to London. She had to work in the housework. But I remember, I was-- must have been eight or nine years old at that moment. They woke-- they woke me up, at night-- early in the morning, when she had to go. But I looked at it as a child, I didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific reason? What did you think then was the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know the reason. For me, the first child, impression you get is why does your mother leave you? A mother should leave a child? That is the first impression. I didn't think it was because of Nazis. A child doesn't think-- a child only sees the distance between the parents and them self-- they-- not the distance, the-- the-- togetherness. And my grandparents stayed with us. So she left with the taxi and she was gone. And when she was gone, I never thought about her anymore. But who I thought about, I directly respected my grandparents, because they were beautiful. A man, he was, my grandfather was 68 or 69, my grandmother, 64 or something. And they stayed with us. But they always looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, indirectly, they were more my parents than my mother. But, still, it was-- there was something unusual happening when she had to leave. It was my mother, and it shocked me, even if I didn't see her much. Like a child loves their mother and loves-- the other way around. But you say the other way around, why? My grandparents never talked about it. They never told us, she had to go. The kept everything even from my father. They never spoke bad about my father. So our grandparents looked after us, and they were the-- the nearest to us, and they love to us. And these were your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were my mother's parents, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do in Europe, at the time? What did they do in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was already retired, but when he was living in Hamburg when I was a very little boy, but I was born there, he was a brokerist. He had four-- Richard-- he had sons, Uncle Richard, he had Uncle Izzy, Uncle Leo, and he had four other daughters, so he had about seven, eight children. In Hamburg, he was a brokerist. He was a well-known businessman-- but always tradition-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very strongly from [NON-ENGLISH], like from Yid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Littman, Joseph and Fannie Littman. Littman, and also the-- my mother was a born Littman. And, also, these three sons, when it came nearer to '36, they left to South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you often had to go on the boat, and they left to South America. Also, as a child, I-- I didn't understand all that. Everybody was leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was before-- '36 was three years before they were- Hitler was really in power and the war started. But coming back to Hamburg, my grandfather was very well-known in Altona. He had a big store where he was like the president, director president. Nicely dressed man. I remember that. In Berlin more, but, in Hamburg, as a child, I didn't see much of it. I also know that one of my uncles were-- had communistical ideas. Jewish people in those days were communistical, like Israel. You know, they have their-- they say even in Israel, we are communists, Zionists.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, I used too march with them in Hamburg as a child. And I used to say sometimes words in front of the Germans, because I heard it from my uncles. I used to say [GERMAN]. And I was only small-- and I used to go on. And my uncle said be quiet, what are you talking about about? Because they were picked up, the communists, you know? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I went with them. That I can remember, I used to go and I used to always ask my uncles when we stood in front of a shop and something, and I saw something under the anti-Semitic, always ask-- like a child asks the questions, what sometimes parents can't answer always. That, I remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how-- how your grandparents and you, in Berlin, fit into the community? Because you said you had neighbors that didn't really like you. How did you fit in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you, my grandfather had his seat in the synagogue. They fit in very well. And they were connected with the-- with what's it called to the [NON-ENGLISH], with the Bundus-- what was it called? In the-- I don't remember so well, but he was already-- at a certain he didn't-- The only he did, my grandfather, Shabbos, he walked a foot to Munchener Strasse, on Saturday, as well to synagogue. I used to go with him. And the Shabbos, and at home, as well, kosher food, you know. Sometimes I sinned. I went to friends next door which were goyims, and I ate something which was not kosher. If my grandfather would have found out, at those days, we were-- I was brought up very strict, but beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you fit in-- how did your family fit into the non-Jewish community? Was there any communication at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was probably in one way respected, because he was beautifully dressed when he went out for Shabbos. They properly said to him [GERMAN]. He didn't-- he's didn't make-- he had friends who came to play cards with him. He had friends from other parts-- all are in his age. They played rummy together. He had his rummy evenings. But the neighbors, where we lived in the house were a lot of Nazis, but not in '36. They didn't show it then, you know. But slowly-- and we didn't ask properly. We were-- I, as a child, sure, I did not, but my grandfather was also probably blinded. Many of them thought, oh, that's going to go over. [GERMAN]-- you know, like now, the anti-Semitism, it's the same thing. But it didn't go over. It went worse and worse. You see the-- my grandparents kept strongly towards also having young children living with them, my sister and me. They didn't want to hurt us much, you see. Probably they knew, but didn't tell us. Probably they thought it's going to go over, and they won't be hurt. They always covered us. They always looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they encourage-- being grandparents and not parents, did they encourage or discourage-- what do you remember relationships with goyims?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't like it, but they had-- had also in the family, they had a daughter, one of their daughter, a Mauritian. My grandfather was married twice, Mauritian married a non-Jew. And colored-- but he was very-- for me, he had themselves a lot-- where they from-- I'm sorry, he came over to the religion, had himself circumcised and all that, you know. But he was a goy. The others all were Jewish married. The sons who left to South America, they were not married then. My mother was with my father, which I didn't know. That parents-- didn't talk good, they didn't talk bad about them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you make a comparison between life then, as you're describing to me in Berlin, and life now in America? Could the relationships be actually compared or be similar, the non-Jewish Jewish relationships, or was there something different?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the states, I mean, life now, I mean-- it's much more freely. The whole world has changed. I mean, people are behaving also-- you know, everything goes so quick. And when I'm in the states, I see the-- the Jewish people, in general, now mixed-- mix into the non-Jews. There's no bother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was clearly a distinction between being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish and not Jewish, there was clearly a separation.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, yes. Definitely, there was. But I was-- at my age, later on, when is good, but definitely. But, sometimes, in families, in life, nobody knows what's happening beforehand. And when my aunt married non-- my grandfather couldn't stop it. He was-- you know that there were those sort of things happen in every family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, he kept quiet about it-- but he was so firm. But I can remember when it used to be Pesach, and used to be Yontiff, they all came-- even the goy son-in-law. And he bought them a lot of presents. He was sitting on the head of the table, he was benching. My grandmother was benching the candles for Pesach. She made her own wine. And my grandfather was very happy. When you have Pesach, a lot of people sit on there-- from the family, on the table, so sons were still there. They didn't immigrate then, that was before '36. So he was a happy man. They came-- and maybe he was happy because he had his little grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was the first time that you actually felt-- you actually felt the first punch of anti-Semitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've got to go back, which is I'm very happy that that happened, that my grandfather could give me-- that he made me bar mitzvah in the Munchener Strasse. I think about it now-- he must've been a happy man. Maybe he felt what's going to happen. He did that. He made me bar mitzvah. I learnt all the Torah. I was-- from the rabbi, chief rabbi, he gave me his blessing. And then I was 13 years of age, and I became a man. And my what's it called, my uncles, already, in '36, they went already. '38 came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister, at 17 years of age, she already worked for Siemens, to help my grandparents to pay the cost, because they were not very-- old people, little small pension. The day came that my uncles sent my grandparents an affidavit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I remember very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They packed some weeks before that, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the day came, the suitcases were standing in the hall. And my grandmother said, I'm not going to leave those kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not going to leave them. My grandfather said, we have to go, they will be look-- no. And they didn't. They didn't leave us. That was a very strong feeling towards me, and maybe my sister, as well. We got so strong together, binded, you know, because of that. I respected them so, so greatly, you see. We still saw my aunts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And '42, already, one family from Hamburg turned-- Finna, and Alpho, and my little cousin Ingrid were already deported. I didn't know then. I didn't know where then, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'd just like to come back, like you said, in '39, when the war started, they were all still there. We were all there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, definitely. Then when the war started, when Hitler became in power, I used to play always on the streets with these non-Jewish people, like I was little boy and I liked playing. And, suddenly, the playing was finished. I went on the street, and my grandparents told me you have to put that star on now. And instead of playing with me, they called me bloody Jew. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to myself, what did I do wrong? Child, at that age, says, well, if you've done something wrong, you know why. But I said what's-- what's-- I went up to my grandfather and said what's this? So, it's not so bad, he said-- it is-- it will go over. Starting at the '39, '40, it got worse. Crystal Night-- Nacht. Tell me Kristallnacht. Tell me about Kristallnacht. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was unbelievable. I walked the streets from Berlin, in [NON-ENGLISH], everywhere, and I saw the window smashing. It was just-- I was standing amongst the people. I said what is this? They're going mad here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing your star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to. I had to. They-- they-- the SR came, made huge stars on the-- Magen David on the windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what? They burnt books, sling them on the street, burnt them. As a child, I said, books? Where we learn from? What's happening? Mad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home. My grandparents, I couldn't talk to because they were-- they probably didn't also know really because they didn't walk the street. And they said, maybe it will be pass. But I schlepped myself about on the streets. Everywhere, every-- I went even to Unter den Linden, when the-- when Hitler was speaking. And I saw him on the balcony. And I was amongst the crowd. Sometimes I took my Magen David off, because we had a Magen David, some of us, with a pin in it. And when we-- when we got out of the district, where nobody knew you, you took it off and put it. And I was standing and I said, they went [NON-ENGLISH] when he came on the balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he stopped-- How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must've been at least 30,000, 40,000. Unter den Linden, maybe more-- it was full. Oh! And he was-- shouting like-- and after, when he went back, they were singing. [SINGING IN GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that's what they were singing. We don't go home, we don't go home until the Fuhrer speaks. And if he speaks, we still don't go home. So I said to myself, what is happening here? I've went home. I said to my grandparents, what's happening? Still they couldn't answer me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we came towards '42, my aunts were deported. But I felt suddenly in me, like something growing, something pushing me. I wanted to escape. I said, at that time, as a small child, 12 years old probably then, just I want to go to Switzerland. I hear from Switzerland. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: But, in my mind, but then I thought, how can I do it? I can't leave my grandparents behind. I can't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had a lot of things in your mind as a child, you-- like you felt already effort-- maybe already, there was something going on, that we are going to die. Without even thinking about it-- we're going to go to Theresienstadt, or Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then, we stayed until '43, and my aunt, who was married with the non-Jew, could stay longer. Coming back as well to my uncle, Uncle Carl. He was-- he made uniforms for the German military officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to visit them. We went to visit them often. When Christmas was, we had to go there, because they were brought up, my cousin, a goy, and we had to sit there with a Christmas tree, which was nice. So we went-- but they had a [NON-ENGLISH], they had where they were tailored. And I saw all the-- the-- from the military, [GERMAN]. As a child, I saw that. I was brought up, I saw this beautiful uniform. So at the moment, I didn't take any notice of it, not going to know what's going to happen later. So then I, suddenly-- there was a whole revolution going on, and in our family- suddenly, my aunt was also deported. My uncle, he was a shikra. He was well in with the SS and with everybody. He went to Hamburg and he must have drank a lot. And he talked, and they-- they found out that his wife was a Jew, and his children. And they deported them. That I remember. The Mauritian was deported before us. And Rita, as well, she was picked up. She had-- but he must have properly talked against her. He must really have talked maybe against the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And him, too, deported or, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was put into prison, suddenly. And we hear it from his secretary who worked there, from the family, that it had happened. Carl and Mauritian, it happened so fast. And you can imagine my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it? Do you remember when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must've been in '42. Because I've got there-- in Birkenau, I found, there, what's it called, when they arrived there. And when that-- maybe if that wouldn't have happened, he could have-- my uncle could have fit in us. But he was quite-- quite rich family, being tailoring for the German, high officers and all that. That was all smashed up. I remember, suddenly, later, '42 and a half, something like that, my grandparents said to me, all the jewelry they had and all of what they had, you know, bring them over to Ilk. That was the associate-- the partner for my uncle, and they should keep it for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, grandmother, what are you talking about? Yes, they should keep it, and put it away somewhere in a safe place. I did that. So what happened, going back, [NON-ENGLISH]. Sometimes, when I came near my district, I had to put it on. At night time, I walked home, sudden-- suddenly somebody hit me. [GERMAN] I said, what? It was getting worse and worse. And what was going on with your education at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Education, there was none. The Jewish schools were closed. And I walked-- the last two years of still staying in Germany, nothing much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be-- what would have done during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I played about. I walked about everywhere. You know, I waked the streets. That's why I saw Kristallnacht. That's why I saw Hitler-- I walked the streets. I saw the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still went to-- there us some-- not schools, but kinderheims, you know, Augustas, I went there. But nothing happened there. There was the Jewish school also in Alexanderplatz, but the-- the teachers were not really giving a proper, what's it called, the schooling, because they-- then-- then they burned the schools, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened then in the synagogues, that was also at the same time as Kristallnacht. They burned that all, and you couldn't go to school. One and a half years of my life in Berlin, I didn't do nothing. My sister continued to work for Siemens, again, a goy-- a non-Jewish family, until-- until the day came, '43. And the first-- and it must be have been already in beginning '43, that suddenly, on the door, [GERMAN]. 4 o' clock in the morning, [GERMAN]. I can remember that. And shouting, [GERMAN]. And two Gestapo came in, in their leather coats, like they're known. [GERMAN]. All you have to leave behind, just hand luggage, and downstairs. Downstairs, my sister was there. My sister went mad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not one neighbor in the house, three floors up, coming down, came out and looked. We said, oh, we didn't see any neighbors. I remember that. We came out the house, the street was suddenly gone. Nobody was there. In the windows, nobody was looking out of the windows. Because the Germans said, we didn't know about this. We got into the van. And then the van we had to get into drove us away. And my sister, about 200 yards farther, jumped the van.
